You may have seen the popular TV show Mad Men, which was centered on an advertising genius named Don Draper. The character of Draper was actually based on a real ad executive named Rosser Reeves. Reeves was legendary for the work he did from the 1940s to the 1980s, especially in the way he differentiated. That is, contrasted his products that he was advertising from their competitors. For example, Reeves came up with the President Dwight D. Eisenhower campaign slogan, I like Ike, because Eisenhower's key differentiator was the fact that the public really liked him. Rosser Reeves also wrote M&M's famous They Melt in Your Mouth Not in Your Hand phrase to contrast that candy's key differentiator from other chocolates. Well, one beautiful spring day, Reeves was walking with a colleague through New York's Central Park. Imagine how beautiful it was with the flowers blooming, the trees budding, and everything fresh and green. As Reeves and his colleague were walking through the park to a restaurant, they passed a man sitting on the sidewalk with a cardboard sign that just said, I am blind, which had attracted only a few coins to the can positioned before the man. After lunch, on their way back to the office, Reeves noticed that the blind man still didn't have much money in his can. So he said to his colleague, I'm going to help this guy. And he reached into his suit coat for what his colleague assumed would be his wallet. But Reeves instead pulled out a pen. He picked up the man's cardboard sign and added just three words to it before replacing it. Rosser and his friend then stood back and watched. The first person who walked by looked at the sign and did a double take. Then she thought for a moment, opened her purse, and put some coins in the blind man's can. The next person did something similar. After 10 minutes, the man's once empty coffee can was nearly overflowing with money. What three words could Reeves have written to change the blind man's situation so dramatic? Take a moment to make a guess. Could it be I am blind? What God sees? Could it be I am blind and you're not? Could it be I am blind? Please, please help? It was none of those. Before the words I am blind on the man's sign, Reeves wrote the words. It's springtime and. That may seem odd at first, but take another moment to think about why those three mere words were so effective. Everyone could enjoy the visual beauty of springtime except the blind man. Reeves brought into consciousness a contrast between the highly unequal experiences of the passer's bye versus the blind man there in the park. The sighted people then became motivated to reduce this inequity in the only way they could in that moment. They gave him some of their resources. Notice that Reeves placed his three words before the blind man's words. I am blind for maximal effect. This reflects one of the mantras of this workshop. What you do first in a situation can change everything that comes next. Rosser Reeves knew this intuitively. He pointed out the unequal contrast up front to influence the decision maker's next response.
